enrichment.short_description: MiroTalk C2C is a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC application for one-to-one cam-to-cam video calls between exactly two participants.
enrichment.seo_title: MiroTalk C2C Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk C2C review: a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC app for encrypted one-to-one cam-to-cam video calls. See features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MiroTalk C2C is an open-source, self-hosted WebRTC web application built specifically for one-to-one, cam-to-cam video calls. It is one of several editions in the MiroTalk WebRTC project family created by developer Miroslav Pejic, alongside MiroTalk P2P (multi-user room calls) and MiroTalk SFU (large-scale group conferencing). Unlike its siblings, MiroTalk C2C deliberately restricts each room to exactly two participants, which the project describes as being for maximum security and an immersive, intimate 1-to-1 real-time communication experience rather than group meetings. The source code is hosted publicly on GitHub under miroslavpejic85/mirotalkc2c and is licensed under the AGPLv3 open-source license, meaning anyone can self-host, inspect, modify, and redistribute it free of charge, subject to copyleft terms. A hosted demo instance is available at c2c.mirotalk.com, allowing users to try the software without deploying their own server, though production use typically involves self-hosting on a VPS or cloud server with Node.js, Nginx, and SSL (Docker deployment is also supported). Architecturally, MiroTalk C2C uses peer-to-peer WebRTC connections, meaning audio and video streams travel directly between the two participants' browsers rather than through a relay server, which minimizes latency and keeps a call's media data outside of any third-party server, a point the project emphasizes for privacy. Users join a call simply by opening a room link and entering a display name, with no downloads, plugins, or account creation required, since it runs entirely in a modern browser via WebRTC. Core features inherited from the broader MiroTalk codebase include end-to-end encrypted media transport, a simple room-ID-based joining flow, and a lightweight, distraction-free calling interface tuned for two-person conversations such as private consultations, tutoring sessions, remote interviews, or telehealth-style check-ins where a dedicated one-to-one link is preferable to a general-purpose meeting room. Because it is free and open source, there is no vendor subscription, per-seat pricing, or company behind it in the traditional SaaS sense; costs are limited to whatever server hosting the operator chooses. Its main competitors are other free peer-to-peer video call tools such as Jitsi Meet, Whereby's free tier, and Google Meet for casual 1:1 calls, as well as its own sibling projects MiroTalk P2P and MiroTalk SFU when more participants or advanced conferencing features (screen sharing, whiteboards, recording, RTMP streaming) are needed. MiroTalk C2C's key differentiator within the MiroTalk family and against generic conferencing tools is its narrow, security-conscious focus on exactly two participants per room, making it a purpose-built tool rather than a general video conferencing platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk C2C is a free, open-source WebRTC application purpose-built for one-to-one, cam-to-cam video calls. It is part of the MiroTalk WebRTC family of self-hosted video calling apps created by developer Miroslav Pejic, sitting alongside MiroTalk P2P (multi-participant room calls) and MiroTalk SFU (scalable group conferencing).

Unlike general-purpose video meeting tools, MiroTalk C2C intentionally caps each room at two participants, framing this as a security and simplicity feature rather than a limitation, and is aimed at direct, private 1:1 conversations rather than team meetings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Because MiroTalk C2C uses direct peer-to-peer WebRTC connections, audio and video travel straight between the two participants' browsers rather than through a central media server, which reduces latency and keeps call data off third-party infrastructure.

Joining a call requires no installation, plugin, or account: users open a room link in a modern browser, enter a display name, and connect. The project emphasizes end-to-end encrypted transport for privacy-sensitive use cases such as consultations or interviews.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MiroTalk C2C is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license. There is no subscription or per-seat fee from the project itself; the only cost is whatever server hosting an operator chooses if they self-host their own instance rather than using the public demo.

A public demo instance is available directly at c2c.mirotalk.com, so casual users can try the tool without any deployment work, while organizations wanting full control typically self-host the open-source code on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: The full source is open source and can be deployed on a private server via Node.js/Docker for complete data control.

Simple, no-download browser-based joining flow
Peer-to-peer architecture keeps media data off third-party servers
Self-hostable for full data control and privacy
Focused, uncluttered design for 1:1 calls
cons: Hard two-participant limit means it cannot be used for group meetings
No official vendor support, SLA, or account management, being community-maintained
Requires technical setup (server, Docker/Node.js) for self-hosting beyond the demo
Fewer built-in collaboration tools (no whiteboard or advanced recording) than the P2P or SFU editions
Documentation and support rely mainly on GitHub issues and community channels

faqs.question: What is MiroTalk C2C used for?
faqs.answer: It is used for private, one-to-one cam-to-cam video calls, such as consultations, interviews, tutoring, or personal conversations, where exactly two people need a secure direct connection.

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk C2C free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, and a public demo is available at c2c.mirotalk.com.

faqs.question: Can more than two people join a MiroTalk C2C room?
faqs.answer: No, each room is deliberately limited to two participants for maximum privacy and simplicity.

faqs.question: How is MiroTalk C2C different from MiroTalk P2P and MiroTalk SFU?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk P2P supports small multi-person rooms and MiroTalk SFU supports large, scalable group conferences using a server-based SFU architecture, while C2C is purpose-built only for two-person calls.

faqs.question: Do I need to install anything to use MiroTalk C2C?
faqs.answer: No, it runs entirely in a modern web browser using WebRTC, with no downloads, plugins, or accounts required.

faqs.question: Can I self-host MiroTalk C2C?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code is publicly available on GitHub and can be self-hosted on your own server using Node.js and Docker.

enrichment.short_description: MiroTalk P2P is a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC video conferencing app that uses peer-to-peer architecture for fast, private small-group calls with screen sharing, chat, and a whiteboard.
enrichment.seo_title: MiroTalk P2P Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk P2P review: a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC video conferencing tool with peer-to-peer calls, screen sharing, chat, and whiteboard.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MiroTalk P2P is the original edition of the open-source MiroTalk WebRTC video conferencing project, created by developer Miroslav Pejic and available at p2p.mirotalk.com and on GitHub at miroslavpejic85/mirotalk. It is a self-hosted, browser-based video calling platform built on peer-to-peer (P2P) WebRTC architecture, meaning participants' audio and video streams connect directly to one another rather than routing through a central media-relay server. This design prioritizes low latency and data privacy, since call media never has to pass through third-party infrastructure, but it also means performance degrades as more participants join a single room, because each participant's device must maintain a direct connection to every other participant. As a result, MiroTalk P2P rooms are best suited to small groups, generally in the range of roughly two to eight concurrent participants, rather than large webinars or all-hands meetings. The project positions itself as a self-hosted, privacy-respecting alternative to commercial tools like Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams. Users can start or join a call directly from a browser with no downloads, plugins, or logins required, and the software supports webcam streaming and screen sharing at resolutions up to 8K and 60fps, an interactive collaborative whiteboard, a public and private chat system with emoji support and message history, file sharing secured with DTLS encryption, and meeting recording of screen, webcam, and audio. MiroTalk P2P is fully open source under the AGPLv3 license, so anyone can deploy their own instance for free using Node.js, Docker, and Nginx, retaining complete control over their data and infrastructure with no vendor lock-in. There is no official paid SaaS tier for this specific edition; operators cover only their own server hosting costs, and some third-party marketplaces resell customized/licensed builds under alternative commercial terms. Its sibling projects, MiroTalk C2C and MiroTalk SFU, extend the same underlying MiroTalk codebase and UI: C2C narrows the scope to strictly two-participant calls, while SFU replaces the peer-to-peer transport with a mediasoup-based Selective Forwarding Unit server architecture to support unlimited participants and larger, more scalable conferences with additional features like RTMP streaming and AI avatars. MiroTalk P2P's core audience is small teams, developers, and privacy-focused organizations that want a free, self-hostable Zoom alternative for small-group video calls without needing the added server complexity of an SFU deployment. Its main competitors include Jitsi Meet, Whereby, and other open-source or freemium WebRTC conferencing tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk P2P is the original, free and open-source edition of the MiroTalk WebRTC video conferencing project, built on a peer-to-peer architecture where participants' browsers connect directly to each other rather than through a central media server.

It is positioned as a self-hosted alternative to Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams for small-group video calls, chat, screen sharing, and whiteboarding, with no download, plugin, or account required to join a room.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The app supports webcam streaming and screen sharing up to 8K resolution at 60fps, a real-time collaborative whiteboard, public and private in-call chat with emoji support and message history, encrypted file sharing, and meeting recording of screen, webcam, and audio.

Because it uses direct peer-to-peer WebRTC connections rather than a server-based relay, rooms perform best with a small number of concurrent participants, roughly in the range of two to eight, rather than large webinars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MiroTalk P2P is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license; anyone can self-host the software on their own server at no licensing cost, paying only for their own infrastructure.

There is no official managed SaaS subscription for this specific edition; users who want a larger-scale or managed deployment typically move to the MiroTalk SFU edition or a third-party licensed build.

features.description: Records screen, webcam, and audio for later reference or sharing.

features.description: File transfer secured using DTLS encryption between participants.

features.title: No download or login required
features.description: Runs entirely in the browser; participants join instantly via a room link.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Fully open-source code that can be deployed on a private server for complete data ownership.

pros: Free and open source under AGPLv3
Low-latency peer-to-peer calls with no central media server
Rich built-in toolset: whiteboard, chat, screen share, recording
No installation or account needed to join a call
Full data control when self-hosted
cons: Peer-to-peer architecture scales poorly beyond small groups compared to SFU-based tools
No official managed hosting or enterprise SLA from the core project
Requires server setup and maintenance knowledge to self-host
Community-driven support rather than dedicated customer service
Large groups should use MiroTalk SFU instead, adding a decision step for new users

faqs.question: What is MiroTalk P2P best used for?
faqs.answer: Small-group video calls, typically up to around eight participants, where low latency and self-hosted privacy matter more than large-scale scalability.

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk P2P free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, self-hostable on your own server at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: How is MiroTalk P2P different from MiroTalk SFU?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk P2P connects participants directly to each other (peer-to-peer), which is efficient for small rooms, while MiroTalk SFU routes media through a server for unlimited participants and better scalability in larger meetings.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to join a MiroTalk P2P call?
faqs.answer: No, participants can join instantly through a browser using a room link and a display name.

faqs.question: Can I record meetings in MiroTalk P2P?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in recording of screen, webcam, and audio.

faqs.question: Can I self-host MiroTalk P2P?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code is available on GitHub and can be deployed on your own server using Node.js and Docker.

enrichment.short_description: MiroTalk SFU is a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC video conferencing platform built on a scalable SFU (mediasoup) architecture for unlimited-participant group calls, webinars, and streaming.
enrichment.seo_title: MiroTalk SFU Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk SFU review: a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC platform for scalable group video conferencing with RTMP streaming and AI avatars.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MiroTalk SFU is the scalable, group-conferencing edition of the open-source MiroTalk WebRTC project created by developer Miroslav Pejic, available at sfu.mirotalk.com and on GitHub at miroslavpejic85/mirotalksfu. Unlike its sibling editions MiroTalk P2P (small peer-to-peer rooms) and MiroTalk C2C (strict two-person calls), MiroTalk SFU is built on a Selective Forwarding Unit architecture powered by the mediasoup WebRTC library. In an SFU model, each participant's media stream is sent once to a central server, which then forwards (relays) it to other participants as needed, rather than requiring every participant's device to connect directly to every other participant as in pure peer-to-peer systems. This server-mediated approach dramatically improves scalability and bandwidth efficiency for larger groups, allowing MiroTalk SFU to support unlimited concurrent rooms and meetings with many more participants than the P2P edition can handle, while still running entirely in the browser with no downloads or plugins required. Positioned as a modern, self-hosted alternative to Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams, MiroTalk SFU offers webcam streaming and screen sharing up to 8K resolution at 60fps with echo cancellation and noise suppression, an interactive collaborative whiteboard, integrated public and private chat, meeting recording, and file sharing. It also includes more advanced capabilities aimed at broadcasting and larger events, such as RTMP streaming to external platforms, virtual backgrounds, ChatGPT integration, and AI avatars for meeting participants. The project is released as free and open-source software under the AGPLv3 license, so organizations can self-host it on their own cloud servers using documented Docker, Node.js, Nginx, and SSL setup scripts, retaining full ownership of their infrastructure and data with no vendor lock-in. For organizations that want alternative licensing terms outside AGPLv3's copyleft requirements, a commercial license of MiroTalk SFU has also been sold through marketplaces such as CodeCanyon for a one-time fee (around $99 as listed), which includes full source code and lifetime updates. MiroTalk SFU's target audience includes businesses, educators, and developers who need to host webinars, large classes, or multi-participant conferences and want an open-source, privacy-respecting, self-hosted platform rather than a proprietary SaaS video conferencing service. Its main competitors are other self-hosted or open-source SFU-based conferencing platforms such as Jitsi Meet and BigBlueButton, as well as commercial tools like Zoom and Google Meet, with MiroTalk SFU differentiating itself through its free open-source licensing, full self-hosting control, and bundled extras like RTMP streaming, ChatGPT integration, and AI avatars not commonly found in comparable free tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk SFU is the scalable group-conferencing edition of the open-source MiroTalk WebRTC project, built on a Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU) architecture using the mediasoup library rather than direct peer-to-peer connections.

This server-mediated design lets it support unlimited concurrent rooms and far more participants per meeting than the peer-to-peer MiroTalk P2P edition, positioning it as a self-hosted, open-source alternative to Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams for larger conferences and webinars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers webcam and screen sharing up to 8K at 60fps with echo cancellation and noise suppression, an interactive whiteboard, integrated chat, file sharing, and meeting recording, all running in the browser with no downloads or plugins.
More advanced capabilities aimed at events and broadcasting include RTMP streaming to external platforms, virtual backgrounds, ChatGPT integration, and AI avatars for participants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MiroTalk SFU is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, allowing anyone to self-host it on their own server infrastructure at no licensing cost.

For teams that want alternative licensing terms outside AGPLv3's copyleft obligations, a commercial license has also been offered through third-party marketplaces such as CodeCanyon as a one-time purchase that includes full source code and lifetime updates.

features.description: Media streams are relayed through a central server rather than connecting every participant directly, enabling much larger meetings than pure P2P.

features.title: Unlimited rooms and participants
features.description: No hard cap on concurrent rooms or call duration, suited to webinars and larger group conferences.

features.description: High-resolution video and screen sharing with echo cancellation and noise suppression.

features.title: RTMP streaming
features.description: Broadcast meetings out to external platforms via RTMP for larger audiences.

features.description: Built-in AI features including avatars and ChatGPT integration for meetings.

features.title: Collaborative whiteboard
features.description: Real-time shared whiteboard for group brainstorming and explanation.

features.title: Meeting recording
features.description: Record screen, webcam, and audio directly from the browser.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Full source code and Docker-based install scripts for deploying on your own cloud server.

pros: Scales to unlimited participants far better than peer-to-peer video tools
Free and open source under AGPLv3, with an optional commercial license for alternative terms
Rich feature set including RTMP streaming, AI avatars, and ChatGPT integration
No download or login required for participants to join
Full data ownership and no vendor lock-in when self-hosted
cons: Self-hosting an SFU server requires more technical setup and server resources than the P2P edition
No official managed cloud SaaS offering from the core open-source project
Community-based support rather than guaranteed enterprise SLAs
Advanced features like AI avatars and ChatGPT integration may require additional configuration or API keys
Running large meetings reliably depends on adequately provisioned server infrastructure

faqs.question: What makes MiroTalk SFU different from MiroTalk P2P?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk SFU routes media through a central server using an SFU (mediasoup) architecture, which scales to unlimited participants, while MiroTalk P2P connects participants directly to each other and works best for small groups.

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk SFU free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, and can be self-hosted at no licensing cost; a commercial license is also available for alternative licensing terms.

faqs.question: Can MiroTalk SFU be used for webinars?
faqs.answer: Yes, its RTMP streaming support and unlimited participant capacity make it suitable for webinars and larger events, not just small meetings.

faqs.question: Does MiroTalk SFU require downloads or plugins?
faqs.answer: No, it runs entirely in the browser using WebRTC, with no downloads, plugins, or logins required to join a call.

faqs.question: What is mediasoup and why does MiroTalk SFU use it?
faqs.answer: Mediasoup is an open-source SFU library that MiroTalk SFU uses to efficiently relay media between many participants, enabling scalable group video conferencing.

faqs.question: Can I self-host MiroTalk SFU?
faqs.answer: Yes, the full source code is on GitHub with documented Docker, Node.js, and Nginx setup scripts for deploying your own instance.

enrichment.short_description: Misago is a free, open-source, Django and React-based forum application designed for building fast, scalable, and modern online communities.
enrichment.seo_title: Misago Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Misago review: a free, open-source Django and React forum application for building fast, modern online communities. Features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Misago is a free and open-source forum application created by developer Rafał Pitoń, with the project's earliest source available under the rafalp/Misago GitHub repository dating back to around 2013. It is built on Python's Django web framework for the backend and JavaScript/React for the frontend, positioning it as a modern, actively developed alternative to other stand-alone forum software such as Discourse, phpBB, or Invision Community. Misago is designed to be fast, scalable, and responsive, targeting communities that want a self-hosted discussion board rather than a hosted SaaS product. Core functionality includes user account management with registration, profile customization, avatars, password recovery, and OAuth login integration with more than 50 third-party providers including Google, Facebook, GitHub, and Steam. Content features support Markdown and BBCode formatting, threaded discussions, post attachments with automatic thumbnail generation, full edit history with revision tracking, and a best-answer marking system for question-and-answer style categories. Community organization is handled through an unlimited number and depth of categories and subcategories, letting administrators structure large or niche communities. Moderation tooling includes a comprehensive moderation queue, post approval workflows, user and content management tools, a banning system that can target usernames, email addresses, or IP addresses, and granular permission controls tied to user roles and groups. As free open-source software, Misago carries no licensing fee: operators install and run their own instance on their own servers, meaning the only costs are hosting infrastructure and any custom development or support they choose to arrange, in contrast to commercial enterprise forum platforms like Jive, Lithium, or Telligent, which historically have run into thousands of dollars per month. The project is distributed via PyPI as the misago Python package and via its GitHub repository, and community discussion and support happen on the official Misago Project Forums (misago-project.org), which itself runs on Misago as a demonstration of the software. Misago's target audience includes developers, open-source communities, niche hobbyist or professional forums, and organizations that want full control over their forum's code, data, and hosting rather than relying on a third-party-hosted community platform. Its main competitors include Discourse (the most prominent modern open-source forum alternative, available both self-hosted and hosted), phpBB, NodeBB, Flarum, and legacy forum software like vBulletin, with Misago differentiating itself through its Django/Python foundation, React-based modern UI, and fully free and open licensing without a paid hosted tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Misago is a free, open-source forum application built on Django (Python) for the backend and React for the frontend, created by developer Rafał Pitoń as a modern, self-hosted alternative to legacy and commercial forum software.
The project aims to provide a fast, scalable, and responsive discussion board experience comparable to platforms like Discourse, while remaining fully open source with no paid hosted tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Misago supports user registration and profiles with OAuth login through more than 50 providers, Markdown and BBCode post formatting, file attachments with thumbnails, edit history with revision tracking, and a best-answer marking system for Q&A-style discussions.

Administrators can build unlimited categories and nested subcategories, and moderators have access to a full moderation queue, post approval workflows, banning tools by name, email, or IP, and role-based permission controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Misago is completely free and open source; there is no license fee to install or run it. Operators are responsible only for their own server hosting and any custom development or support they choose to arrange.

This contrasts with commercial enterprise forum platforms that can cost thousands of dollars per month, making Misago attractive to budget-conscious communities and developers who want full control over their forum's code and data.

Full control over hosting, data, and customization
cons: Requires self-hosting and server management skills, unlike hosted forum SaaS products
Smaller ecosystem and plugin marketplace compared to Discourse
No official paid support tier or enterprise SLA from a vendor
Documentation and community size are more limited than larger open-source forum projects
Requires familiarity with Python/Django for deeper customization

faqs.question: Is Misago free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Misago is free and open-source software; there is no license fee, only the cost of hosting it yourself.

faqs.question: What technology stack does Misago use?
faqs.answer: Misago's backend is built on Django (Python) and its frontend uses React (JavaScript).

faqs.question: Who created Misago?
faqs.answer: Misago was created by developer Rafał Pitoń, with the project's history on GitHub dating back to around 2013.

faqs.question: How does Misago compare to Discourse?
faqs.answer: Both are modern open-source forum platforms; Misago is Django/Python-based and fully self-hosted with no paid hosted tier, while Discourse is Ruby-based and offers both self-hosted and paid managed hosting options.

faqs.question: Does Misago support social login?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports OAuth login integration with more than 50 providers including Google, Facebook, GitHub, and Steam.

faqs.question: Can Misago handle large communities?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports unlimited categories and subcategories and is designed to be fast and scalable for growing communities.

enrichment.short_description: MISP is a free, open-source threat intelligence platform for collecting, storing, correlating, and sharing cybersecurity indicators of compromise and threat information between organizations.
enrichment.seo_title: MISP Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MISP review: a free, open-source threat intelligence platform for sharing indicators of compromise between security teams. Features, pricing, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MISP (originally short for Malware Information Sharing Platform, now generally referred to simply as MISP - Open Source Threat Intelligence Platform) is a free, open-source software project for collecting, storing, correlating, and sharing cybersecurity threat indicators and threat intelligence between organizations and communities. The project began around May 2011 when developer Christophe Vandeplas, frustrated that indicators of compromise (IOCs) were commonly shared informally via email or unstructured PDF documents rather than in machine-parsable formats, started building a proof of concept using the Django-adjacent PHP framework CakePHP. Development and maintenance today are led primarily by CIRCL (the Computer Incident Response Center Luxembourg), the national CERT/CSIRT of Luxembourg, working alongside contributors from Belgian Defence, NATO's NCIRC (Computer Incident Response Capability), and a broad international open-source community. The project has received funding support from the European Union, including through the Connecting Europe Facility program, as well as from CIRCL itself. MISP is released as free and open-source software under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL), meaning organizations can self-host their own instance, inspect and modify the source code, and are not required to pay any license fee. Architecturally, MISP functions as a central repository and correlation engine for threat data: security teams ingest indicators (IP addresses, file hashes, domains, malware samples, vulnerability data, and more) as structured events and attributes, and the platform automatically correlates related indicators across events, supports tagging via taxonomies, and enables both automated and manual sharing of intelligence with trusted partner organizations or sharing communities through MISP's peer-to-peer synchronization protocol. Core features include a flexible data model for threat events and attributes, built-in correlation and enrichment, support for open standards like STIX for interoperability with other threat intelligence tools, a REST API and PyMISP Python library for automation and integration with SIEMs and other security tooling, community/organization-based access controls and distribution levels for controlling who can see shared intelligence, and curated MISP feeds and communities that let organizations subscribe to external threat data. Because it is free and open source, there is no official vendor-set pricing tier; costs are limited to self-hosting infrastructure, though the ecosystem includes many free public and vetted MISP communities as well as commercial vendors offering managed MISP hosting or support. MISP's primary audience is security operations centers (SOCs), CERTs/CSIRTs, government cybersecurity agencies, financial sector ISACs, and enterprise threat intelligence teams that need to share and correlate indicators of compromise at scale. Its main competitors and alternatives include commercial and open-source threat intelligence platforms such as OpenCTI, Anomali, ThreatConnect, and Recorded Future, with MISP differentiating itself through its fully free, open-source, community-governed nature and its wide adoption as a de facto standard for structured threat intelligence sharing among government and defense-affiliated CERTs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MISP is a free, open-source threat intelligence platform used to collect, store, correlate, and share cybersecurity indicators of compromise and other threat data between organizations. The project began around 2011, created by developer Christophe Vandeplas to solve the problem of threat indicators being shared informally via email or unstructured documents rather than machine-parsable formats.

Today MISP is developed and maintained primarily by CIRCL, the Computer Incident Response Center Luxembourg, together with contributors from Belgian Defence, NATO's NCIRC, and an international open-source community, with funding support that has included the European Union's Connecting Europe Facility program.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MISP structures threat data as events and attributes, automatically correlating related indicators across the platform and supporting tagging via taxonomies for consistent classification. It supports open standards such as STIX for interoperability and provides a REST API plus the PyMISP Python library for automating integrations with SIEMs and other security tools.

Organizations can control who sees shared intelligence through community and organization-based access controls and distribution levels, and can synchronize with trusted partners or subscribe to curated public MISP feeds and communities for external threat data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MISP is released under the AGPL open-source license and is free to download, self-host, and modify, with no vendor license fee. Costs are limited to whatever infrastructure an organization uses to host its own instance.

A broader ecosystem exists around MISP including free public threat-sharing communities and commercial vendors that offer managed MISP hosting, integration, or support services for organizations that prefer not to operate the platform themselves.

features.title: Event and attribute data model
features.description: Structures threat indicators as events with attributes, enabling consistent, machine-parsable threat data.

features.title: Automatic correlation
features.description: Correlates related indicators across events to surface connections analysts might otherwise miss.

features.title: STIX and open standards support
features.description: Interoperates with other threat intelligence tools via widely used data exchange standards.

features.title: REST API and PyMISP library
features.description: Enables automation and integration with SIEMs and other security infrastructure.

features.title: Community and distribution controls
features.description: Fine-grained control over which organizations or communities can see shared intelligence.

features.title: Peer-to-peer synchronization
features.description: Instances can sync threat data with trusted partner organizations automatically.

features.title: Taxonomies and tagging
features.description: Standardized tagging for classifying and filtering threat data consistently.

features.title: Curated feeds and communities
features.description: Subscribe to external, vetted threat intelligence feeds and sharing communities.

pros: Free and open source with no license fees
Widely adopted as a de facto standard among CERTs, government agencies, and SOCs
Strong correlation engine for connecting related threat indicators
Extensive integration options via REST API and PyMISP
Active international community and ongoing EU-supported development
cons: Requires self-hosting and security operations expertise to deploy and maintain
User interface and onboarding can be complex for newcomers compared to modern commercial TIPs
No official vendor SLA or dedicated commercial support from the core project itself
Data quality depends heavily on the communities and feeds an organization chooses to trust
Scaling and performance tuning for very large deployments requires specialized knowledge

faqs.question: What does MISP stand for?
faqs.answer: MISP originally stood for Malware Information Sharing Platform and is now commonly referred to as an open-source threat intelligence platform.

faqs.question: Is MISP free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MISP is free and open-source software released under the AGPL license, with no vendor license fee.

faqs.question: Who maintains MISP?
faqs.answer: MISP is primarily maintained by CIRCL, the Computer Incident Response Center Luxembourg, along with contributors from Belgian Defence, NATO NCIRC, and an international open-source community.

faqs.question: What kind of organizations use MISP?
faqs.answer: Security operations centers, CERTs/CSIRTs, government cybersecurity agencies, financial sector ISACs, and enterprise threat intelligence teams commonly use MISP.

faqs.question: Does MISP support automation and integrations?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides a REST API and the PyMISP Python library for integrating with SIEMs and other security tooling.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to MISP?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include OpenCTI, Anomali, ThreatConnect, and Recorded Future, though MISP is distinguished by being fully free, open source, and community-governed.

enrichment.short_description: Missive is a shared team inbox and internal chat platform that lets teams collaborate on email, SMS, and social messages from a single app.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 3 users); paid plans from $14/user/month billed yearly
enrichment.seo_title: Missive Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Missive review: a shared team inbox and chat platform for collaborating on email, SMS, and social messages. See features, pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Missive is a shared team inbox and internal communication platform that combines collaborative email management with built-in team chat, tasks, and automation. It was founded in 2015 in Quebec City, Canada, by Philippe Lehoux, Etienne Lemay, and Rafael Masson, who used profits from an earlier startup, Conference Badge (an event registration tool), to bootstrap Missive without external venture capital funding, making it a fully self-funded, profitable company. The product connects to shared email addresses (such as support@ or sales@ inboxes), SMS numbers, and social/messaging accounts, letting multiple team members view, discuss, assign, and respond to the same conversations from one interface. Its core differentiator is embedding real-time internal chat directly inside email threads, allowing teammates to discuss how to respond to a message without leaving the conversation or switching to a separate tool like Slack. Other core features include shared draft collaboration (seeing teammates typing a reply in real time), conversation assignment and tagging, tasks and to-do management tied to conversations, customizable automation rules for routing and labeling messages, integrations with external tools, an API for custom workflows, and (on higher tiers) AI-assisted drafting and analytics/reporting. Missive reports serving more than 25,000 users across more than 4,000 companies. Pricing follows a freemium SaaS model: a free plan supports teams of up to 3 users with core shared-inbox and chat functionality; the Starter plan is priced at $14 per user per month (billed yearly) for up to 5 users with one shared inbox, internal chat, and basic rules; the Productive plan is $24 per user per month (billed yearly) for up to 50 users, adding unlimited shared inboxes, more advanced automation rules (up to 1,000), integrations, basic analytics, and API access; and the Business plan is $36 per user per month (billed yearly) with unlimited users, SAML SSO, IP restrictions, advanced analytics, SOC 2 Type II-related compliance features, and personalized onboarding. All paid plans include a 30-day free trial with no credit card required, and monthly billing is available at a premium over annual pricing. Missive's target audience is small to mid-sized teams, particularly customer support, sales, and operations teams, that need multiple people to collaborate on a shared email address without juggling shared logins or forwarding threads manually. Its main competitors include Front, Help Scout, Hiver, Zendesk, and Gmail-based shared inbox tools, with Missive differentiating itself through its combination of a shared inbox and full internal chat/task system in a single native app, its bootstrapped/independent ownership structure, and its multi-channel support spanning email, SMS, and social accounts rather than email alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Missive is a shared team inbox and internal chat platform designed for teams that collaborate around email, SMS, and social messages. Founded in 2015 in Quebec City, Canada, by Philippe Lehoux, Etienne Lemay, and Rafael Masson, the company is fully bootstrapped without venture capital funding.

The product's core idea is to combine a shared inbox with real-time internal team chat embedded directly inside email threads, letting teammates coordinate on responses without switching to a separate messaging app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Missive supports shared email, SMS, and social accounts, conversation assignment and tagging, shared draft collaboration where teammates can see each other typing responses in real time, and tasks tied directly to conversations.
Higher tiers add automation rules for routing and labeling messages, integrations with external tools, an API for custom workflows, AI-assisted drafting, and analytics/reporting for team performance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Missive offers a free plan for teams of up to 3 users with core shared-inbox and chat features. Paid plans are Starter at $14 per user per month (up to 5 users), Productive at $24 per user per month (up to 50 users), and Business at $36 per user per month (unlimited users), all billed yearly with monthly billing also available.

Every paid plan includes a 30-day free trial with no credit card required, and pricing scales with added features like unlimited shared inboxes, advanced automation, SSO, and priority support at higher tiers.

features.description: Multiple teammates can view, discuss, and respond to the same email, SMS, or social conversations from one shared address.

features.title: In-thread internal chat
features.description: Real-time team chat embedded directly inside email conversations for discussing how to respond.

features.title: Shared draft collaboration
features.description: Teammates can see each other typing a reply in real time before it's sent.

features.title: Conversation assignment and tagging
features.description: Assign conversations to specific team members and organize them with labels.

features.title: Tasks tied to conversations
features.description: Turn emails or messages into trackable tasks without leaving the conversation.

features.title: Automation rules
features.description: Automatically route, label, or assign incoming messages based on custom rules.

features.title: Multi-channel support
features.description: Manage email, SMS, and social/messaging accounts from a single shared inbox app.

features.title: AI-assisted drafting
features.description: AI features help draft replies faster on eligible plans.

pros: Combines shared inbox and internal team chat in one native app
Bootstrapped, profitable company independent of VC pressure
Free plan available for very small teams
Multi-channel support beyond just email, including SMS and social accounts
Real-time shared draft visibility reduces duplicate replies
cons: Per-user pricing can add up for larger teams compared to some flat-rate alternatives
Free plan is limited to just 3 users
Higher-tier features like SSO and advanced analytics are gated behind the most expensive plan
Primarily desktop/web and mobile app focused, without the extensive third-party app marketplace some competitors offer
Best value requires annual billing; monthly billing costs more per seat

faqs.question: What is Missive used for?
faqs.answer: Missive is used as a shared team inbox and internal chat tool, letting multiple people collaborate on shared email, SMS, and social message accounts from one app.

faqs.question: Is Missive free?
faqs.answer: Missive offers a free plan for teams of up to 3 users; paid plans start at $14 per user per month billed yearly.

faqs.question: Who founded Missive?
faqs.answer: Missive was founded in 2015 in Quebec City, Canada, by Philippe Lehoux, Etienne Lemay, and Rafael Masson.

faqs.question: Does Missive have internal team chat?
faqs.answer: Yes, Missive embeds real-time internal chat directly inside email threads so teams can discuss responses without leaving the conversation.

faqs.question: How is Missive different from Slack or Gmail?
faqs.answer: Unlike Slack, Missive is built around shared inboxes for email/SMS/social conversations rather than general team messaging, and unlike Gmail, it adds native internal chat, task management, and multi-user collaboration on the same inbox.

faqs.question: Does Missive offer a free trial for paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, all paid plans include a 30-day free trial with no credit card required.

use_cases.description: Small companies managing multiple shared addresses and channels without a dedicated helpdesk system.

enrichment.short_description: Misskey is a free, open-source, decentralized microblogging platform in the Fediverse that lets anyone run their own federated social network instance.
enrichment.seo_title: Misskey Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Misskey review: a free, open-source, decentralized microblogging platform in the Fediverse. Learn its features, self-hosting, and alternatives like Mastodon.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Misskey is a free and open-source, decentralized microblogging and social networking platform that is part of the Fediverse, the network of interconnected servers that communicate using the ActivityPub protocol. It was created in 2014 by Japanese software engineer Eiji Shinoda, known online as syuilo, who originally built it as a BBS-style internet forum while still a high school student. After Shinoda introduced a timeline feature, the project evolved into the microblogging platform it is known as today. In 2018, Misskey added support for the ActivityPub protocol, transforming it from a standalone social platform into a federated one capable of interacting with other ActivityPub-compatible software such as Mastodon and Pleroma. The flagship and largest Misskey instance, misskey.io, launched on April 15, 2019, and became a major hub for Japanese-language Fediverse users; Misskey saw a significant surge in adoption in 2023 as users looked for Twitter/X alternatives, with misskey.io's registered accounts reportedly more than doubling from roughly 180,000 in July 2023 to about 370,000 by August 2023. Misskey's source code is released under the AGPLv3 open-source license and is developed primarily in TypeScript on both the Node.js backend and the frontend, coordinated through the misskey-dev organization on GitHub. Because Misskey is decentralized, there is no single company or central server that all users must join; instead, anyone can self-host their own Misskey instance and communicate with users on other Misskey instances or other Fediverse platforms, similar in spirit to email federation. On Misskey, user posts are called notes, which support a much longer character limit than traditional microblogging (commonly up to 3,000 characters by default, and configurable per instance), along with attachments including images, videos, audio, and polls. Distinctive features that set Misskey apart from Mastodon and other Fediverse software include custom emoji reactions on posts (rather than simple likes), channels for topic-based discussion spaces, antennas that let users build custom keyword- or account-based timelines, a built-in Drive feature for managing uploaded files, extensive UI theming and customization, configurable user roles, and a responsive web interface built as a Progressive Web App. Because Misskey is free, open-source, self-hosted software rather than a single commercial product, there is no vendor pricing model; individuals or organizations that want to run an instance are responsible for their own server hosting costs, while joining an existing public instance like misskey.io is typically free to end users. Misskey's target audience includes privacy- and decentralization-minded social media users, Fediverse enthusiasts, Japanese-language online communities where it has particularly strong adoption, and developers interested in running or customizing their own federated social platform. Its main competitors and closely related alternatives include Mastodon (the most widely known Fediverse microblogging platform), Pleroma, Akkoma, and forks of Misskey itself such as Sharkey and Firefish/Calckey, with Misskey differentiating itself through its distinctive reaction-based interaction model, extensive customization options, and strong roots in the Japanese Fediverse community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Misskey is a free, open-source, decentralized microblogging platform that is part of the Fediverse, the network of federated social servers connected via the ActivityPub protocol. It was created in 2014 by Japanese developer Eiji Shinoda (syuilo), initially as a BBS-style forum, later evolving into a microblogging platform after adding a timeline feature.

In 2018 Misskey adopted ActivityPub, enabling federation with other platforms such as Mastodon, and its flagship instance misskey.io launched in April 2019, later becoming one of the largest hubs of the Japanese-language Fediverse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Posts on Misskey, called notes, support up to roughly 3,000 characters by default (configurable per instance) along with images, videos, audio, and polls. The platform is distinguished by custom emoji reactions rather than simple likes, topic-based channels, and antennas for building custom keyword- or account-based timelines.

Additional features include a built-in Drive for managing uploaded files, extensive UI theming and customization, configurable user roles and permissions, and a responsive Progressive Web App interface, all developed in TypeScript under the AGPLv3 open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Misskey itself is free, open-source software with no vendor pricing model; anyone can self-host an instance at their own infrastructure cost. Joining an existing public instance such as misskey.io is typically free for end users.
Because there is no central company selling Misskey, costs vary entirely based on whether a user joins an existing free instance or chooses to run and maintain their own server.

features.title: ActivityPub federation
features.description: Interoperates with other Fediverse platforms like Mastodon, letting users on different servers follow and interact with each other.

features.title: Custom emoji reactions
features.description: Users react to notes with custom emoji rather than a simple like button, a signature Misskey interaction model.

features.title: Channels
features.description: Topic-based discussion spaces within an instance for organizing conversations by subject.

features.title: Antennas
features.description: Custom timelines built from specific keywords, users, or lists for tracking topics of interest.

features.title: Drive file management
features.description: A built-in file manager for organizing images, videos, and other uploads.

features.title: Extensive UI theming
features.description: Deep customization of the interface's appearance and layout.

features.title: Configurable user roles
features.description: Instance admins can define custom roles and permissions for users.

features.title: Long-form notes
features.description: Supports post lengths up to roughly 3,000 characters by default, longer than traditional microblogging limits.

pros: Free and open source under AGPLv3 with no vendor lock-in
Decentralized architecture avoids reliance on a single company or server
Distinctive, expressive interaction model with custom emoji reactions
Highly customizable UI, themes, and user roles
Strong, active community, especially in the Japanese Fediverse
cons: Running your own instance requires server administration knowledge
Fragmented ecosystem across many independent instances can be confusing for new users
No official company support or SLA since it is community-maintained
Content moderation quality varies significantly between instances
Feature set and terminology (notes, antennas, drive) differ from mainstream social apps, adding a learning curve

faqs.question: What is Misskey?
faqs.answer: Misskey is a free, open-source, decentralized microblogging platform that is part of the Fediverse, using the ActivityPub protocol to federate with other servers like Mastodon.

faqs.question: Who created Misskey?
faqs.answer: Misskey was created in 2014 by Japanese developer Eiji Shinoda, known online as syuilo.

faqs.question: Is Misskey free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Misskey is free, open-source software released under the AGPLv3 license; joining a public instance is typically free, and self-hosting costs only your own server infrastructure.

faqs.question: How is Misskey different from Mastodon?
faqs.answer: Both are Fediverse platforms using ActivityPub, but Misskey has a distinct feature set including custom emoji reactions, channels, antennas, and a Drive file manager, along with strong roots in the Japanese Fediverse community.

faqs.question: What is the character limit for posts on Misskey?
faqs.answer: Posts, called notes, default to up to roughly 3,000 characters, though this limit can be customized by individual instance administrators.

faqs.question: Can I run my own Misskey server?
faqs.answer: Yes, Misskey is self-hostable open-source software, and anyone can deploy their own instance and federate with the wider Fediverse.

use_cases.title: Decentralized social networking
use_cases.description: Users seeking an alternative to centralized platforms like Twitter/X who want a federated, community-run experience.
use_cases.audience: Privacy- and decentralization-minded social media users

use_cases.title: Niche and interest-based communities
use_cases.description: Groups running their own themed instance with custom moderation and branding.

use_cases.title: Japanese-language Fediverse participation
use_cases.description: Users and communities engaging in Japan's particularly active Misskey and Fediverse ecosystem.

features: Complete control over moderation and customization

enrichment.short_description: MistServer is a fully open-source, public-domain media streaming server that supports a wide range of protocols including HLS, DASH, RTMP, WebRTC, and SRT.
enrichment.seo_title: MistServer Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MistServer review: a free, open-source, public-domain streaming media server supporting HLS, DASH, RTMP, WebRTC, and SRT. Features, pricing, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MistServer is a streaming media server toolkit that supports a wide range of protocols and formats for ingesting, transcoding, and delivering live and on-demand video, including HLS, MPEG-DASH, RTMP, WebRTC, SRT, and others. The project traces its roots to 2009, when Dutch company DDVTech B.V. was founded in the Netherlands after a media-related project failed due to shortcomings in the streaming server software available at the time; the founding team asked whether they could build something better themselves, and MistServer was the result, undergoing private development from roughly 2009 to 2012 before wider release. For years, MistServer used a mixed licensing model, with core functionality released under the AGPLv3 open-source license while certain advanced features remained proprietary and were sold under commercial licenses (including a Pro tier historically priced around $2,500 per month) to fund ongoing development. In 2021, DDVTech and the MistServer project were acquired by Livepeer, Inc., and in 2022 Livepeer released MistServer into the public domain. Following this, Livepeer offered to sell DDVTech back to MistServer's lead engineer for a nominal amount, and the team collectively decided to restructure as a cooperative, forming a new parent company, OptiMist Video B.V., with each team member as an equal part-owner. On February 14, 2022, with the release of MistServer 3.0, the entire codebase became fully open source under a public-domain dedication (with Zero-Clause BSD as a fallback license in jurisdictions that do not recognize public-domain dedications), removing all software-imposed licensing restrictions and making every feature free to use, including for commercial purposes, with no per-server or per-feature charges. Architecturally, MistServer is designed as a modular, high-performance toolkit: it can ingest streams via numerous input protocols, transcode and package them, and output to numerous player-facing protocols simultaneously, making it suitable for building custom live-streaming and video-on-demand platforms, CDNs, and broadcast workflows. The company behind it today (variously referred to via mistserver.com and mistserver.org) continues to offer paid professional services on top of the free open-source core, including Service Level Agreements (SLAs), technical assistance, and custom feature development, typically billed as a simple monthly service charge or custom licensing arrangement rather than per-project or per-hour fees, with current professional pricing available on request via their contact/sales channel. MistServer's target audience includes broadcasters, streaming platform operators, telecom and media companies, and developers building custom video infrastructure who need protocol flexibility and full control over their streaming stack rather than relying on a closed commercial CDN/streaming SaaS product. Its main competitors include commercial streaming server software such as Wowza Streaming Engine, as well as open-source alternatives like nginx-rtmp, SRS (Simple Realtime Server), and cloud-native streaming/CDN services from major cloud providers, with MistServer differentiating itself through its fully public-domain licensing (unusual among streaming server software), broad protocol support, and cooperative ownership model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MistServer is a media streaming server toolkit built to ingest, transcode, and deliver live and on-demand video across a wide range of protocols, including HLS, MPEG-DASH, RTMP, WebRTC, and SRT. Its roots trace back to 2009, when Dutch company DDVTech B.V. was founded after a failed media project exposed shortcomings in existing streaming server software.

After a 2021 acquisition by Livepeer, Inc., the entire MistServer codebase was released into the public domain in 2022 with the launch of version 3.0, and the original team restructured as a cooperative, OptiMist Video B.V., with each team member an equal part-owner.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MistServer functions as a modular toolkit that can accept input via numerous streaming protocols and output to numerous player-facing protocols simultaneously, making it suitable for building custom live-streaming platforms, video-on-demand services, CDNs, and broadcast workflows.
Because it is public-domain software, every feature is free to use for any purpose, including commercial use, with no per-server or per-feature restrictions imposed by the software itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core MistServer software is entirely free, full-featured, and usable for any purpose, including commercial use, with no software-imposed restrictions since its full open-sourcing in 2022.

The team behind MistServer also offers optional paid professional services on top of the free core, such as SLAs, technical assistance, and custom feature or build development, typically arranged as a simple monthly service charge or custom licensing agreement, with current pricing available by contacting their sales team.

features.title: Broad protocol support
features.description: Supports HLS, MPEG-DASH, RTMP, WebRTC, SRT, and other streaming protocols for both ingest and output.

features.title: Public-domain licensing
features.description: Fully open source under a public-domain dedication (with Zero-Clause BSD fallback), free for any use including commercial.

features.title: Live and on-demand streaming
features.description: Handles both live broadcast workflows and video-on-demand delivery within the same toolkit.

features.title: Modular architecture
features.description: Components can be combined to build custom streaming pipelines suited to specific infrastructure needs.

features.title: Transcoding support
features.description: Can transcode streams to different formats and bitrates as part of the delivery pipeline.

features.title: No per-server or per-feature limits
features.description: All features are available without artificial software-imposed restrictions.

features.title: Optional professional services
features.description: SLAs, technical assistance, and custom development available from the maintaining team for a fee.

pros: Fully public-domain licensed, free for any use including commercial
Wide protocol support covering nearly every major streaming standard
No artificial feature gating or per-server licensing costs
Cooperative ownership model with an experienced founding team
Suitable for building custom, high-performance streaming infrastructure
cons: Requires technical expertise to deploy, configure, and maintain compared to managed streaming SaaS products
No fixed public pricing for professional support services; requires contacting sales
Smaller community and ecosystem compared to some commercial streaming platforms
Historical documentation still references older mixed open/proprietary licensing that no longer fully applies
Best suited to organizations building their own infrastructure rather than those wanting an out-of-the-box hosted solution

faqs.question: Is MistServer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the entire MistServer codebase has been fully open source and public domain since 2022, free to use for any purpose including commercial use, with optional paid professional services available separately.

faqs.question: What protocols does MistServer support?
faqs.answer: MistServer supports a wide range of streaming protocols including HLS, MPEG-DASH, RTMP, WebRTC, and SRT, among others, for both ingest and output.

faqs.question: Who created MistServer?
faqs.answer: MistServer originated from Dutch company DDVTech B.V., founded in 2009, and is now maintained by OptiMist Video B.V., a cooperative formed by the original team after a 2021-2022 acquisition and public-domain release by Livepeer.

faqs.question: Can I use MistServer commercially for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, since the 2022 public-domain release with MistServer 3.0, all features can be used commercially with no software-imposed licensing fees.

faqs.question: Does MistServer offer paid support?
faqs.answer: Yes, the maintaining team offers optional professional services such as SLAs, technical assistance, and custom development for a fee, with current pricing available on request.

faqs.question: What is MistServer used for?
faqs.answer: It is used to build custom live-streaming and video-on-demand infrastructure, including broadcast platforms and CDNs, by broadcasters, media companies, and developers.

use_cases.title: Custom live-streaming platforms
use_cases.description: Developers building their own branded streaming service that needs broad protocol support.

use_cases.title: Broadcast and media workflows
use_cases.description: Broadcasters and media companies needing flexible ingest and delivery across multiple protocols.

use_cases.title: Video-on-demand infrastructure
use_cases.description: Organizations building VOD delivery pipelines without relying on a closed commercial CDN product.

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: Monthly service charge or custom agreement
